mechanism_narrative: Sustained positive month-over-month growth in non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates a robust and expanding labor market. This strength supports consumer confidence and spending, which in turn drives corporate revenues and leads to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 55% of instances where PAYEMS shows positive month-over-month growth over a 12-month period.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) rises above a specific threshold, it signals an aggressive monetary tightening cycle by the central bank. This increases borrowing costs for businesses and consumers, dampens economic activity, and reduces corporate profitability, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 generates positive 60-day returns in more than 40% of periods when FEDFUNDS is above 3.0% for three consecutive months.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous and significant decline in both retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) indicates a broad-based weakening of consumer demand and investment. This synchronized downturn signals an impending economic slowdown, negatively impacting corporate earnings and S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 shows positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of instances where both RSAFS and HOUST decline by the specified thresholds.

mechanism_narrative: In an environment of already low unemployment, a further decline in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals an overheating labor market. This can lead to wage inflation and increased pressure on corporate profit margins, eventually prompting monetary tightening and leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 continues to rally, producing positive 20-day returns in more than 60% of periods when the 3-month average UNRATE is below 4.0%.

mechanism_narrative: A significant deceleration in industrial production (INDPRO) growth, falling below a critical expansion threshold, indicates weakening manufacturing activity and broader economic slowdown. This directly translates to lower corporate earnings expectations and negatively impacts S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 shows strong positive 5-day returns in more than 50% of instances when industrial production growth is significantly slowing below 0.5% over three months.

mechanism_narrative: Persistent, moderate inflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL), indicates a healthy level of aggregate demand in the economy. This allows companies to maintain pricing power and profit margins, supporting positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 produces negative 60-day returns consistently (more than 60% of the time) when CPIAUCSL shows moderate year-over-year growth (e.g., between 1.5% and 3.0%).

mechanism_narrative: Strong real GDP growth (GDPC1) combined with moderate, stable inflation (CPIAUCSL) creates a 'goldilocks' economic scenario. This environment fosters robust corporate earnings without triggering aggressive monetary tightening, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 YoY growth > 2.5% AND CPIAUCSL YoY growth between 1.5% and 3.0%
failure_condition: The S&P 500 delivers negative 20-day returns in more than 50% of periods despite strong GDP growth and moderate inflation meeting the specified criteria.

mechanism_narrative: During periods of economic recovery following a recession, strong and accelerating job creation (PAYEMS growth) signals a return to economic health and consumer confidence. This rebound in economic activity drives a recovery in corporate earnings and leads to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 fails to show positive 5-day returns in more than 55% of instances during periods of strong employment recovery (PAYEMS growth > 0.5% over three months).
